Eﬀicient Timing Prediction and Optimization Using Derivable Gradient  
Boosting Machine Model at Placement Stage

YUSHAN WANG, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan, China

hps://doi.org/10.1145/3780100

.
Eﬀicient Timing Prediction and Optimization Using Derivable  
Gradient Boosting Machine Model at Placement Stage  
XU HE, Information science and Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, China  
YUSHAN WANG, Hunan University, Changsha, China  
CHENJING YANG, Hunan University, Changsha, China  
RENJUN ZHAO, Hunan University, Changsha, China  
YIBO LIN, School of Integrated Circuits, Peking University, Beijing, China  
PEIYU LIAO, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, Hong Kong  
BEI YU, CSE, the Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, Hong Kong  
YAO WANG, Independent Researcher, Changsha, China  
CHANG LIU, National University of Defense Technology, Changsha, China  
YANG GUO, Computer, National University of Defense Technology, Changsha, China  
Timing closure is a critical but eﬀort-taking task in VLSI designs. In this paper, we investigate timing models and their  
associated optimization techniques suitable for integration into early design process. For accurate timing analysis at placement  
stage, an innovative timing prediction model that can be transformed into a derivable function is proposed, serving as a  
replacement for the conventional Elmore delay. One signiﬁcant advantage of derivable timing models is their ability to be  
formulated as mathematical functions. This enables the derivation of gradients, which can be explored as direct timing  
optimization guidance during design process. Therefore, we further present a timing optimization method through model  
integration into iterative placement process. Experimental results demonstrate that our derivable timing model provides  
accurate prediction performance. Additionally, when integrated into the placement stage, it outperforms the use of Elmore  
delay and non-derivable models, achieving 12%∼24% smaller Total Negative Slack (TNS) and Worst Negative Slack (WNS),  
with an average wirelength reduction of 1%∼5%.  
Additional Key Words and Phrases: Machine Learning, Static Timing Analysis, Timing Prediction, Timing Optimization,  
Placement  
Xu He is the corresponding author (Email: dawn.hx@gmail.com).

ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
2
•
X. He et al.  
1
Introduction  
Timing closure is a critical yet challenging task in VLSI design. Timing optimizations are performed throughout  
the entire design process. The earlier the intervention stage it involves in, the greater the room it has for timing  
optimization. Today, many timing optimization techniques are applied at early stages of design. For instance,  
during the placement stage, various methods have been proposed, i.e., net-based and path-based algorithms.  
However, aside from modifying the process algorithms for timing-driven optimization, it has also been observed  
that the accuracy of the timing model can signiﬁcantly impact timing performance.  
Accurate timing estimation before sign-oﬀ stage, i.e., at placement stage, is challenging due to the absence of  
essential connection physical information. As a result, traditional timing analysis methods rely on simpliﬁed circuit  
models, which reduce accuracy signiﬁcantly as wire lengths and fan-outs grow. To address timing closure under  
inaccurate timing analysis, circuits are designed using a worst-case assumption, which adds an over-pessimistic  
timing margin to ensure the performance speciﬁcation under process variations. Industrial experience has shown  
that, the immense gap between the performance predicted by EDA tools and the ﬁnal silicon performance can  
be up to 30% [34]. In addition to the pessimistic timing margin, in early design stage, a large arbitrary clock  
uncertainty is inserted in order to cover up the net delay estimation “error” before routing. Consequently, the  
design might be optimized with a large mis-correlation with the sign-oﬀ net delay information, which could  
result in multiple design turn-around iterations and/or poor sign-oﬀ Power-Performance-Area (PPA) trade-oﬀs.  
To overcome the accuracy limitations of early timing analysis, recent methods have utilized machine learning  
(ML) models like random forest [  
6
,
22], XGBoost [9], and Graph Neural Network (GNN) models [20]. These  
techniques have shown improved timing prediction accuracy.  
However, a key challenge remains: integrating these accurate ML models into the design ﬂow for practical  
performance evaluation. Most existing timing-driven algorithms rely on the Elmore model for timing analysis.  
Although the Elmore model is known to be inaccurate, it continues to be widely used in design tools for preliminary  
timing analysis due to its simple mathematical formula, which allows for computational eﬃciency and facilitates  
the derivation of timing optimizations in various timing-driven placement algorithms [  
7
,
8
,
11, 19, 28, 29, 32,  
32 33, 33, 35]. Therefore, to enable machine learning (ML) to provide more accurate timing optimization, ML  
,
models must not only improve their accuracy but also incorporate integration methods that can guide timing  
optimization eﬀectively.  
In this work, we aim to enhance both accurate timing analysis and corresponding optimization guidance  
method for timing model in placement stage, leading to more eﬃcient circuit designs as shown in Fig. 1. Our focus  
is on developing a solution that combines the accuracy of ML-based model with the computational eﬃciency and  
derivability advantages of the Elmore model. Firstly, to match the beneﬁts of the Elmore model, we propose a  
novel timing prediction model based on Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM). Similar to the Elmore model, our  
GBM model can be transformed into a derivable function. To enhance model accuracy, we generate a lookahead  
RC network during the placement stage for essential timing feature extraction. To further assess the eﬀectiveness  
of our proposed timing model, we give the model integration method. As the model derivation can identify timing  
beneﬁts related to connection criticality, the critical path can be optimized based on the partial derivative of our  
GBM model with respect to wirelength. In our implementation, the GBM model is integrated into the open-source  
placer DREAMPlace [30], employing net-weighting technology as a study case for timing optimization evaluation.  
Our experiments demonstrate that, compared to previous net-weighting methods, our model and its integration  
signiﬁcantly reduce unnecessary wirelength adjustments during timing optimization, leading to superior Total  
Negative Slack (TNS) and Worst Negative Slack (WNS) results.  
The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows:  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
Eﬀicient Timing Prediction and Optimization Using Derivable Gradient Boosting Machine Model at Placement Stage  
•
3
Fig. 1. Accurate timing analysis can reduce over-pessimistic design.  
•
We introduce a derivable function-based timing prediction model that can easily replace the traditional  
Elmore model. Signiﬁcantly, its derivative provides direct and guiding inﬂuence on timing optimization,  
adding a crucial dimension to its applicability and eﬀectiveness.  
•
•
We propose a novel feature extraction method based on look-ahead RC network, which compensates for  
the absence of routing information in early placement process.  
We propose a pin-to-pin weighting method that can directly identify critical pin-to-pin connections within  
the critical path. This approach minimizes resource wastage when optimizing the multi-pin network along  
the critical path.  
Experimental results have demonstrated that, under an industry advanced process technology (28nm),  
our machine learning-based delay model has achieved above 0.99 correlation with the sign-oﬀ timing  
on average. Moreover, the proposed timing model and integration approach in timing-driven placement  
can eﬀectively improves timing performance while reducing wirelength loss caused by overly pessimistic  
predictions.  
•
The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 gives the motivation and previous related works  
on timing prediction and optimization techniques. Section 3 and Section 4 give the problem formulation and  
framework overview, respectively. In Section 5 and Section 6, we discuss the details of our timing model and  
integration method. Experimental results and conclusions are presented in Section 7 and Section 8, respectively.  
2
Motivation & Related Works  
STA is a critical component of the chip design ﬂow, invoked repeatedly to evaluate timing performance and  
identify critical paths. Especially in the early design stages, both timing accuracy and optimization guidance are  
crucial aspects of the prediction model when integrating it into existing timing-driven workﬂows.  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
 
4
•
X. He et al.  
Fig. 2. Elmore delay model.  
Currently, Elmore model [14], or the so-called ﬁrst moment of the impulse response, remains the dominant  
choice for timing analysis during early design stages, such as placement process. This is due to its simple  
expression as a resistance-capacitance (RC) function, implicitly dependent on wirelength. Fig. 2 gives an RC  
chain example. Elmore delay can be considered as ﬁnding the delay through each segment , as the segment  
ꢀ
resistance ꢁꢀ times the downstream capacitance, and then taking the sum of the delays from the root to the sink.  
Segment number equals to the edge number of a path, e.g., segments from input pin to output pin. Elmore delay  
ꢂ
of this RC chain is given in Equation (1). Given a routing result, each net can be modeled as an RC tree. For tree  
structure, the Elmore delay computation is similar, in which the downstream capacitance of node  
capacitance of all its branches.  
ꢀ
is the sum of  
ꢁ
ꢁ
∑
∑
ꢃꢄꢅꢆꢇꢈ(ꢉꢊꢋ) =  
ꢁꢀ ×  
ꢌꢂ .  
(1)  
ꢀ=1  
ꢂ=ꢀ  
It is observed that the Elmore model simpliﬁes delay calculation by expressing it directly as a function of  
resistance  
placement algorithms to translate the optimization of critical path delay into the optimization of wirelength. For  
example, net-weighting methods [ 28 29 35] assign higher weights to timing-critical nets during placement. This  
prioritizes shorter wirelengths for these critical connections, ultimately reducing their delays and achieving timing  
ꢁ
and capacitance  
ꢌ
, both of which have a linear relationship with wirelength. This allows timing-driven  
8
,
,
,
optimization. Other techniques, like path-based techniques [  
7
,
11, 19] directly model path timing constraints  
during placement, allowing for explicit optimization by manipulating cell coordinates.  
While the Elmore model is highly integrable into the placement process, it suﬀers from inaccuracy compared to  
sign-oﬀ tool, particularly as wirelength increases or the number of fanouts in a net grows. Typically, wire delays  
are more challenging to calculate than gate delays due to their black-box nature. As shown in Fig. 3, statistical  
analysis reveals that as the timing path length increases, the contribution of interconnection wire delays becomes  
increasingly signiﬁcant. Consequently, inaccuracies in delay estimation, especially for wire delays, cannot be  
ignored. For example, in some cases, compared to sign-oﬀ wire delay, the Elmore delay error can exceed 800ps, as  
illustrated in Fig. 3. Such inaccuracies can lead to incorrect critical-path timing evaluations and negatively aﬀect  
timing optimization during placement.  
In addition to the Elmore model, more accurate models have been proposed, such as the D2M (Delay with 2  
Moments) delay metric [5], which accounts for the second moment of the impulse response, as well as other  
complex timing models like the Arnoldi model and the AWE (Asymptotic Waveform Evaluation) model, typically  
used at the post-routing stage. However, regardless of the method used, the issue of incomplete timing information  
before sign-oﬀ remains. This leads to discrepancies between the timing analysis conducted before sign-oﬀ and  
the ﬁnal timing results after sign-oﬀ.  
To narrow the gap between timing analysis and the sign-oﬀ golden result, machine learning methods can be  
used for timing prediction. Related work includes: delay prediction in the logic synthesis stage [31  
,
36  
,
37]; timing  
prediction in the placement stage [  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
6,  
20, 22]; wire delay prediction in the routing stage [9]; cross-tool prediction  
   
Eﬀicient Timing Prediction and Optimization Using Derivable Gradient Boosting Machine Model at Placement Stage  
•
5
Fig. 3. The large gap between Elmore delay and sign-oﬀ wire delay may result in a significant inaccurate timing guide for  
critical path identification.  
in the sign-oﬀ stage [21 24] , Signal Integration (SI) mode timing prediction [25] and Path-Based Analysis (PBA)  
,
timing prediction [18, 26], etc.  
For timing analysis during the placement stage, prior prediction approaches encounter the following critical  
challenges: (1) Lack of model-integration methodology: The primary goal of timing analysis at this stage is  
to optimize timing during placement. However, the feasibility of replacing the traditional Elmore model with  
ML models needs further exploration, particularly regarding the integration method of the repeated model  
invocations during optimization. (2) Trade-oﬀ between model accuracy and design pessimism: The accuracy  
of timing models is aﬀected by several factors, such as the selection of appropriate RC information features and  
the choice of ML algorithm. Over-simpliﬁed models may reduce prediction accuracy, resulting in pessimistic  
design risks. Conversely, more complex models often introduce signiﬁcant computational overhead, resulting in  
substantial time costs when utilized frequently.  
Both timing accuracy and optimization guidance are crucial aspects of the prediction model. Therefore, we  
aim to provide a timing prediction model that not only oﬀers accurate predictions but also makes it easier to  
integrate into the placement optimization process. In this work, we evaluate our model on placement optimization  
guidance by integrating it into the net-weighting technique as a case study. It is important to note that the model  
derivability allows for its application to other timing optimization techniques, such as path-based methods.  
3
Problem Formulation  
In the problem formulation, besides timing accuracy evaluation, it is also crucial to incorporate timing considera-  
tions into the design ﬂow. The term timing “slack” is commonly used to determine a critical timing path, which  
is deﬁned as the diﬀerence of the required arrival time minus the arrival time. Negative slack means the path  
arrival time does not meet the requirement. The problem formulation of derivable timing model and integration  
for early timing optimization at placement stage can be described as follows:  
Problem. Given netlists and input layouts, cell library, and timing reports generated by the golden sign-oﬀ timer,  
the objective is to train a derivable delay predictor that minimizes the discrepancy between the predicted delay  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
 
6
•
X. He et al.  
Fig. 4. Our timing analysis and optimization framework at placement stage.  
and the golden timing report. Moreover, for new netlist, the timing model can be eﬀectively integrated into the  
placement process for timing optimization, i.e., total negative slack (TNS) and worst negative slack (WNS).  
4
Methodology Overview  
Fig. 4 provides an overview of our methodology. Our approach primarily consists of two parts: generating an  
accurate derivable timing prediction model, and integrating it into the placement optimization process to fully  
utilize its derivability. The modules related to timing, including model construction, analysis, and optimization,  
are shown in red. Relative to our earlier version in [22], the key diﬀerences, namely the derivable timing model  
and its corresponding optimization guidance, are highlighted in blue.  
To generate derivable model, Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM) is utilized to predict the net delay. We transform  
this machine learning model into a derivable function form by creating derivable basic learners within GBM.  
This approach not only provides an eﬃcient timing model, but also serves as a foundation for subsequent timing  
optimization during placement. In the model training process, given the placement input and technology libraries,  
we have to do the feature extraction for the net delay model. At the placement stage, the routing information is  
still unavailable. To improve the accuracy of timing prediction, we apply a fast routing analysis on the inputted  
placement layout to generate a look-ahead RC tree for each net. With the help of the generated RC network,  
the topology-related characteristics like wire-length, as well as the parasitic parameters like the resistance and  
capacitance of nets, can be extracted and fed to train the net delay model. The training dataset for each net  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
 
Eﬀicient Timing Prediction and Optimization Using Derivable Gradient Boosting Machine Model at Placement Stage

7
Fig. 5. Feature extraction example. For a multi-pin net  
to sinks and

3, it can be partitioned into two-pin nets from input pin  
ꢍ
of driver  
ꢎ
1
ꢏ
ꢐ
, respectively. Each two-pin net-based delay incorporates both driver gate delay and wire delay to the target  
sink. Based on the placement layout, a fast routing analysis is applied to generate a look-ahead RC tree of  
timing features are extracted according to the generated RC tree.  
ꢂ3. Finally, the  
with timing label is extracted from a post-routing sign-oﬀ timing report using the PrimeTime, with the crosstalk  
considered and the timing analysis performed in signal integrity mode.  
Essentially, since our model is derivable, to further validate the derivation eﬀectiveness on timing optimization  
guidance, we adopt the net-weighting framework as a case study of model integration. During the model  
integration into the placement process, once the initial placement is provided, timing analysis is repeatedly  
invoked to guide timing optimization on the intermediate layout. For each net, given its timing features, the  
trained derivable model is utilized for the prediction of the net delay, followed by path timing computation. In  
the net-weighting approach, a proper net-weighting should assign higher weights on more timing-critical nets,  
with the hope that the placement engine will reduce the lengths of these critical nets and thus their delays to  
achieve better overall timing. Here, the interconnect length is derived using the aforementioned timing model,  
which allows for the calculation of timing derivation to determine the wirelength weight.  
5
Derivable Timing Prediction  
In this work, the objective of our timing analysis is to improve the accuracy of the traditional timing model, while  
keeping its characteristics of convenient usage to combine with or even replace traditional model in prior timing  
optimization algorithms. The key techniques include the generation of the look-ahead RC network, the feature  
selection & extraction method, and the derivable ML algorithm.  
5.1 Look-ahead RC Network Generation  
At the placement stage, the routing information is still unavailable. To improve the accuracy of timing prediction,  
we apply a fast routing analysis to generate a look-ahead RC network for the input placement layout. In such a  
way, not only the wirelength, but also the parasitic resistance and capacitance of the nets can be obtained for the  
correlation improvement of net delay with the post-routing results.  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
 
8
•
X. He et al.  
Table 1. Feature Summary in Our derivable Model  
Type  
Feature  
Reference  
Netlist  
Driving Strength  
Fanout Number  
Output Load  
Driver  
-Related  
Look-Ahead RC Network  
Cell Libaray,  
NLDM Gate Slew  
NLDM Gate Delay Look-Ahead RC Network  
Distance  
Elmore Delay  
Sink Slew  
Placement Result  
Interconnection  
-Related  
Look-Ahead RC Network  
To do fast routing analysis, we ﬁrst partition each multiple-pin net into two-pin nets using Steiner tree  
algorithm [12]. Each two-pin net is then routed by L-shape routing. This routing analysis can be replaced by  
other simpliﬁed global routers as well for more sophisticated routing analysis.  
5.2 Feature Extraction  
In timing prediction, feature selection is of critical importance to the eﬀectiveness of model application. The  
look-ahead RC network is applied for timing feature extraction. Table 1 lists the features for our net-based delay  
model.  
Each net-based sample is a connection between a driver input pin and a target sink, i.e., ꢍ → ꢏ in Fig. 5, the  
features can be divided into driver-related features and interconnection-related features.  
The driver-related features include: (1) Driving strength: It is extracted by the cell type, and it determines the  
output impedance of the driver. Typically, the higher the driving strength, the smaller the net delay. (2) Output  
load: It is the load capacitance of driver. (3) Fanout number: The number of sink pins of the net. (4) NLDM gate  
slew: It represents the speed of rise and fall transitions, and thus inﬂuences timing. (5) NLDM gate delay: It is the  
delay of the timing arc within the driving gate. It should be mentioned that, both gate slew and gate delay are  
not the golden result from the sign-oﬀ timer. Instead, they are obtained by the NLDM look-up table with two  
input indexes: the input slew and the output load of the driver, both of which are computed with the help of our  
look-ahead RC network.  
The interconnection-related features include: (1) Distance: It is the Manhattan distance between the locations  
of the driving output pin and the target sink pin. (2) Elmore delay: We select the capacity and resistance from the  
RC tree, and compute the Elmore delay between the driving output pin and the target sink pin. (3) Sink slew: The  
total slew of all sink pins, along with its associated impulse and capacitive load, is calculated by the Elmore delay  
model. It represents the quality of the signal transmission.  
Fig. 5 gives an example of feature extraction. Given a net  
ꢂ
3, we do routing analysis by partitioning 3 into  
ꢂ
two-pin nets ꢍ → ꢏ and ꢍ → ꢐ using Steiner tree algorithm. Each two-pin net is then routed by L-shape routing.  
A look-ahead RC tree can be generated from the routing analysis and the cell library. The features of each two-pin  
net are then extracted. The extracted features of the two-pin net ꢍ → ꢏ are given in Fig. 5 as an example.  
It should be mentioned that, to consider rising and falling delays in the same predictor, for each two-pin net,  
diﬀerent data samples are generated according to the combinations of rising and falling signals, i.e., at most  
2
×
2
=
4 combinations. For each two-pin net, its rising and falling samples are varied by diﬀerent related-features  
and timing labels.  
More timing features can further optimize timing prediction, but would require more feature computation  
and increase model complexity. To do the trade-oﬀ between timing performance and complexity, only the above  
timing features are selected in our placement.  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
 
Eﬀicient Timing Prediction and Optimization Using Derivable Gradient Boosting Machine Model at Placement Stage  
•
9
Fig. 6. Our derivable GBM driver-sink delay model with MLP as basic learner.  
5.3 Derivable GBM Model  
We choose the GBM model, a kind of decision tree-based algorithm, for its proven eﬀectiveness with tabular  
and discrete data [17], making it well-suited for pin-to-pin and net-based timing predictions, i.e. XGBoost,  
LightGBM. However, common GBM methods are not derivable, which, to some extent, constrains their usability  
and adaptability in the context of timing optimization during the placement process. To address this limitation,  
the soft gradient boosting machine (sGBM) framework [16] is applied. This framework ingeniously connects  
multiple derivable base learners into a cohesive, derivable system. It achieves this by introducing the local losses  
and the global losses, drawing inspiration from the GBM concept.  
Our derivable basic learner makes use of the multilayer perceptron (MLP). MLP is a modern feedforward neural  
network composed of distinct layers, which includes at least an input layer, a hidden layer, and an output layer.  
At its core, MLP relies on three fundamental elements: weights, biases, and activation functions. The MLP model  
is essentially a function made up of multiple nonlinear and linear transformations. Importantly, each of these  
individual transformations is derivable, ensuring that the entire function expression is also derivable.  
Speciﬁcally, assuming that a MLP contains an input layer, a hidden layer, and an output layer, the MLP function  
can be expressed as Equation (2).  
ꢑꢒꢓ(ꢐ ,ꢔ) =ꢕꢃ · ꢖℎ (ꢕℎ ꢐ + ꢍℎ ) + ꢍꢃ,  
(2)  
1
1
1
ꢄ

1
2

1
activation function. Th1e ꢕꢃ and ꢍꢃ are the 1 × ꢂ weight matrix and bias value of the output layer.  
1

ꢑꢒꢓ(ꢐ ,ꢔ) =ꢕꢃ · ꢖℎ (ꢕℎ (. . . ꢖℎ (ꢕℎ ꢐ + ꢍℎ ) . . . )

In our GBM model, its corresponding function can be expressed as the sum of the outputs of all basic MLP  
leaners. The function expression of our GBM model is given in Equation (4).

∑
ꢘꢈꢄꢙꢚꢛꢜꢑ(ꢐ ) =ꢄꢇ ·

ꢝꢆꢐ ꢋꢞꢄꢊꢟ(ꢠ) =ln(1 + eꢅ),  
where  
ꢂ
is the number of basic MLP learner, and ꢄꢇ is the learning rate, which controls the contribution of each  
base learner to the overall model. The choice of activation function is very important to the MLP, and various  
activation functions are examined in our experiments, including the commonly used ꢝꢀꢡꢅꢆꢀꢏ, ꢢꢙꢂℎ, and ꢁꢈꢄꢊ  
functions. However, both ꢝꢀꢡꢅꢆꢀꢏ and ꢢꢙꢂℎ functions suﬀer the problem of gradient vanishing, and the ꢁꢈꢄꢊ  
function is not derivable at all points. To solved the problems, we have turned to the ꢝꢆꢐ ꢋꢞꢄꢊꢟ function, which is  
a smoothed version of ꢁꢈꢄꢊ function. The derivative of the ꢝꢆꢐ ꢋꢞꢄꢊꢟ function is the ꢝꢀꢡꢅꢆꢀꢏ function, for which  
does not suﬀer from the gradient vanishing issue fortunately, thereby resolves all the aforementioned problems.  
6
Timing Optimization with Derivable Model  
In addition to model accuracy, we also investigate the corresponding timing optimization method by utilizing the  
derivability of the model. In this study, we adopt the net-weighting technique to demonstrate timing optimization  
using derivable timing model, as net-weighting technique is commonly employed in timing-driven placement  
and also applicable to other non-derivable model for performance comparison.  
In net-weighting technique, it assigns diﬀerent weights to diﬀerent nets such that the placer minimizes the  
weighted sum of net wirelength shown in Equation (5).  
Í
min  
ꢆ,ꢇ  
ꢣ(ꢈ) ·ꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ;ꢤ,ꢚ) + ꢥꢘ(ꢤ,ꢚ),  
(5)  
ꢈ∈ꢉ  
where x, y are cell coordinates, ꢣ(ꢈ) is the weight of net  
a density penalty term with the density penalty weight  
is solved by gradient descent on the objective function.  
ꢈ
, and the density constraint is usually transformed into  
ꢥ
. In an analytical placement, this optimization problem  
Although the net-weighting technique appears to be straightforward, generating an eﬀective net-weighting  
scheme is still not an easy task. Assigning bigger weights to critical nets typically reduces their wirelength  
and delays, but it can also lead to length growth and worsen timing of other near-critical nets, therefore the  
emergence of new critical path. To solve the problem, we introduce a dynamic net-weighting method, which  
involves updating weights based on the above derivable timing model during placement process.  
Many dynamic net-weighting techniques primarily set weights for the whole netsꢈ ∈ ꢃ as shown in Equation (5).  
For those methods, if only part of the driver-sink interconnections of multi-pin net  
ꢈ
belong to the critical paths  
with negative slacks, the net weight ꢣ(ꢈ) is updated according to the worst slack. However, when dealing with a  
multi-pin net  
ꢈ
that involves multiple driver-sink connections, it becomes necessary to determine whether these  
connections is truly a critical path or not, and then should be optimized explicitly if it is. For this reason, it’s  
crucial to assign diﬀerent weights to the lengths of driver-sink connections from diﬀerent paths to accurately  
reﬂect their importance in the overall timing.  
In our implementation, we add two-pin pseudo-nets to the driver-sink connections for whose worst path slacks  
are below zero. For a two-pin pseudo-net , its net weight computation is given in Equation (6), in which the  
ꢈ
belonging path slack and delay of the pseudo-net ꢈ are considered.  
ꢊꢋ ꢌ(ꢈ)  
(6)  
ꢣ(ꢈ) =  
× ꢣꢀꢇꢈꢁꢙꢋꢀꢆ(ꢈ),  
ꢊꢍꢎꢆ  
whereꢣꢀꢇꢈꢁꢙꢋꢀꢆ(ꢈ) is the wire and gate delay ratio of driver-sink pseudo-net  
ꢈ
, in which the gate delay is obtained  
from the cell library. The wire delay can be calculated by subtracting the gate delay from the predicted driver-sink  
net-based delay. Alternatively, the ML model can be directly adjusted to predict the wire delay speciﬁcally for the  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
     
Eﬀicient Timing Prediction and Optimization Using Derivable Gradient Boosting Machine Model at Placement Stage  
•
11  
timing arc from the driver output to the sink input. The ꢏꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ) is applied to account for the impact of timing  
delay change to wirelength ꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ) by partial derivative on ꢘꢈꢄꢙꢚꢛꢜꢑ(ꢐ ), and ꢏꢑꢙꢤ is the maximum derivative  
of all pseudo-nets.  
For a MLP learner in GBM model with one hidden layer as an example, using the timing delay model  
ꢘꢈꢄꢙꢚꢛꢜꢑ(ꢐ ) with features  
ꢐ
of  
ꢈ
in Equation (4), the wirelength impact can be estimated in Equation (7).  
For each MLP learner in GBM model with multiple hidden layers, it can be derived similarly.

ꢏꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ) =  
ꢦꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ)

ꢦꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ)  
Referring to the feature vector  
ꢐ
, wirelength mainly involves the Elmore delay and distance. For feature  
distance of  
ꢈ
, its partial derivative with respect to ꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ) is 1. For feature Elmore delay ꢃꢄꢅꢆꢇꢈ(ꢈ), the partial  
derivative is given in Equation (8) [35].  
ꢑꢉꢒꢓꢃꢔꢈ (ꢈ)  
= ꢇꢎ ·ꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ) + ꢎꢁꢊ, +ꢇꢌꢒ,  
(8)  
ꢑꢋ ꢌ(ꢈ)  
where  
ꢇ
and  
ꢎ
are the unit length wire resistance and capacitance, respectively, ꢁꢊ is the output resistance of the  
driver, and ꢌꢒ is the load capacitance.  
It implies that given a speciﬁc technology setting with trained GBM model, large weights on wirelength-related  
features, long wire with weak driver and large load will exhibit higher sensitivity to the same wirelength change.  
For other features, i.e., gate slew, since they are tabular or discrete data and cannot be derived by wirelength,  
their partial derivative with respect to ꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ) is simply set to 0. Above net-weighting of driver-sink connections  
can be extended to handle more general timing model by similar computation of deriving features with respect  
to ꢕ ꢒ(ꢈ).  
7
Experimental Result  
The proposed placer approach is implemented in C++17 and timing model trained with Python 3.8, and then  
evaluated on a workstation with an Intel Xeon Core (@3.20 GHz) and 256 GB DDR4 memory. The experiment  
setups and result analysis are illustrated and discussed in detail as follows.  
7.1 Dataset Generation  
To evaluate the generalization ability of timing prediction and optimization, we implement existing open-source  
RTL designs to generate golden timing samples through the complete RTL-to-GDSII ﬂow. This includes ITC’99  
benchmark circuits [13] and open-source RISC-V circuits such as CV32  
[
1], CVA6  
[
2
], and XuanTie Open C910  
[3],  
all synthesized and implemented using a 28nm industry process. All circuit designs are ﬁrst synthesized using  
Synopsys Design Compiler. Physical design (P&R) is then conducted using Cadence Innovus, followed by parasitic  
extraction with Synopsys StarRC. Finally, Synopsys PrimeTime is executed in signal integrity mode to generate  
timing reports as the ground truth.  
Among the open-source RISC-V CPUs, CV32 is a 32-bit RISC-V core with a 4-stage pipeline, CVA6 is a 64-bit  
Linux-capable 6-stage RISC-V core with a 32KB L1 data cache and a 16KB L1 instruction cache, and C910 is a  
high-performance 64-bit multi-core processor featuring 64KB L1 instruction and data caches along with a 1MB  
L2 cache. These RISC-V CPUs diﬀer signiﬁcantly from the ITC’99 circuits, exhibiting greater complexity and  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.

•

2,310,629 2,338,764 313,806 20,532,192  
The circuit data is the total number of all implementations.  
larger sizes. By utilizing these datasets, we aim to assess the eﬀectiveness of timing prediction and optimization  
in handling more challenging and realistic designs.  
Since each implementation of an ITC’99 circuit is small in scale, it is not ideal for evaluating prediction models  
because prediction accuracy can ﬂuctuate signiﬁcantly. For the same reason, CV32, although slightly larger than  
the largest ITC’99 case, remains a relatively simple RISC-V circuit. To obtain suﬃcient net samples, we variate  
several tool parameters and generate 4 diﬀerent design implementations for ITC’99 circuits and 3 diﬀerent  
implementations for circuit CV32. This dataset enrichment approach has also been adopted in prior work [6]. In  
contrast, CVA6 and C910 are much larger than ITC’99 and CV32. Their larger size not only produces a greater  
number of net samples but also provides substantial diversity in sample characteristics, such as wirelength and  
fanout range, which is suﬃcient for evaluating prediction performance. Therefore, for these large-scale circuits,  
we did not generate additional implementations.  
A summary of the circuit characteristics is provided in Table 2, including the total number of samples across  
all implementations, where each sample refers to a pin-to-pin connection. Half of the samples from circuits CV32,  
CVA6 and C910 are randomly selected for training, while the remaining samples are used for testing. This includes  
the entire dataset of ITC’99 for cross-validation. There is no intersection between training and testing data.  
It should be mentioned that, in the dataset generation process from RTL to sign-oﬀ, netlist may change across  
diﬀerent stages. It is impractical to track the netlist changes from placement-stage to sign-oﬀ stage to produce  
dataset, as only a very small number of nets remain identical between these two stages. Instead, we extract  
physics-related information from the sign-oﬀ netlist that is equivalent in scope to the information obtainable at  
the placement stage. A dataset is then constructed from the sign-oﬀ netlist along with the corresponding sign-oﬀ  
timing labels. To ensure consistency with the features available at the placement stage, all post-placement details  
in the sign-oﬀ netlist are excluded.  
7.2 Timing Prediction Validation  
7.2.1 Delay Prediction Analysis. To demonstrate the eﬀectiveness of our “DelayGBM” model, we conduct a  
comparative analysis against the “Elmore” delay model and prior timing prediction models, i.e., XGBoost-based  
model “MLCAD’22” [10] and our earlier work “DAC’22” [22] based on Random Forest (RF). It is important to  
note that all previous models were developed using their proposed features and methodologies within our timing  
predication framework, where rising and falling arcs are considered separately. Additionally, since the “DAC’22”  
work uses a net-based approach, we ensure fairness by adding the driving gate delay to both the Elmore delay  
and the wire delay [10].  
The comparison on the net-based delay is given in Table 3. The columns “Mean” (ps), “Max” (ps), and “Cor” are  
the mean error, maximum error, and correlation with respect to the sign-oﬀ timing from PrimeTime, respectively.  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.

The column “CPU” presents the inference run-time in seconds. In our implementation, the look-ahead RC network  
generation takes up about 14% of total inference run-time averagely, in which the Steiner tree construction  
accounts for more than half of it. Normally, the larger a net is, the longer run-time it takes. The training time for  
“DelayGBM” is 1543 seconds for the total 11.5M training data, respectively.  
As shown in Table 3, all methods achieve a “Cor” greater than 0.95, making it diﬃcult to diﬀerentiate their  
performance. This is due to the small circuit scale of ITC’99, which results in short wirelength and a wire  
delay proportion of less than 5% relative to gate delay. Since wire delay analysis is generally considered more  
challenging than gate delay analysis, we intentionally increase the circuit chip size and reduce cell density to  
lengthen cell distances and thereby increase the proportion of wire delay. In the following performance analysis,  
we use dataset derived from physical implementations with sparse cell distribution, thereby presenting a more  
challenging scenario characterized by increased wire delay.  
In Table 4, as the wire delay proportion increases, the accuracy of the Elmore delay decreases signiﬁcantly,  
while the “Cor” metric of ML-based models decreases by less than 8%. Among these models, the average “Cor” of  
“DelayGBM” remains above 0.95. This high consistency with the sign-oﬀ delay eﬀectively avoids the pessimistic  
estimations seen with the Elmore delay. It is also worth noting that “DelayGBM” demonstrates a prediction  
performance equivalent to or even better than previous non-derivable models, indicating that a derivable learning  
model can be a more suitable substitute for non-derivable models in timing prediction.  
Error distributions of the delay predictions are depicted in Fig. 7 for the cross validation results on CVA6  
and C910. Results on other circuits and designs are similar. It is shown that our prediction errors are generally  
concentrated near ±0 with small spread, which indicates the eﬀectiveness of our model.  
We also analyzed other related works on timing prediction. For instance, the results of the work [9] is very  
similar to those of “MLCAD’22”, with a correlation diﬀerence of less than 0.01, and thus are not listed due to  
table space constraints. In work [6], the timing analysis is overly simpliﬁed, such as assuming the maximum  
delay for driver with multiple input pins and not diﬀerentiating between rising and falling delays, instead using  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
   
14  
•
X. He et al.  
Fig. 7. Comparison of error distribution of net delay prediction on circuit CVA6 and C910.  
the worst-case scenario instead. These simpliﬁcations lead to signiﬁcant pessimism in the predictions, resulting  
in low sign-oﬀ correlation below 0.8.  
To assess the eﬀectiveness of the selected features for timing prediction, we extend the feature set beyond those  
listed in Table 1 by incorporating several commonly used timing-related features: (1) Segment-Num: the number  
of edges from the output to the input in the RC network; (2) Context-Elmore: for a given target sink, the other  
sinks are deﬁned as context sinks, and the sum of Elmore delays between the driving pin and these context sinks is  
referred to as Context-Elmore; (3) D2M: the second moment of the impulse response; (4) Sink-Cap: the total input  
capacitance of all sinks in a net; (5) RUDY: the Rectangular Uniform wire Density of a net, serving as a routing  
congestion indicator; and (6) HPWL: the half-perimeter of the bounding box of the RC network. Experimental  
results indicate that the overall correlation remains nearly unchanged compared to the baseline feature set, with  
variations limited to within  
±
0
.
003 across diﬀerent cases. However, incorporating these additional features into  
the full softGBM set introduces extra computational overhead, increasing inference runtime by approximately  
30%.  
7.2.2 Path Timing Analysis. We integrate the wire delay prediction results into OpenTimer [23] for path timing  
analysis. By traversing the path in topology order level-by-level, the endpoint arrival time can be obtained. The  
overall predictions of the endpoint arrival time are summarized in Table 5. Compared with other methods, our  
slack errors are the lowest, and our average correlation is about 0.94.  
As shown in Fig. 8 for CVA6 and C910, compared to the Elmore and “MLCAD’22” methods, the distributions of  
our arrival path predictions closely align with the PrimeTime results, as indicated by the solid line, demonstrating  
a better match with the golden results from PrimeTime.

Fig. 8. Analysis of endpoint arrival time on circuit CVA6 and C910.  
A key application of timing analysis is the identiﬁcation of timing-critical paths with negative endpoint slacks.  
Path slack is deﬁned as the diﬀerence between the required time and the arrival time at an endpoint, such as a  
ﬂip-ﬂop (FF) or a primary output. The slack value is crucial to identify violated paths that require further timing  
optimization. In our experiments, slacks greater than a threshold, i.e., -15ps in PrimeTime report, are classiﬁed  
as non-critical paths, while negative slacks below this threshold are classiﬁed as critical paths. This is because  
minor negative violations can be easily corrected through cell library retiming, and the margins provided by  
on-chip variation (OCV) and clock uncertainty settings during veriﬁcation ensure that such small violations do  
not result in functional failures.  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.

The results of the critical paths classiﬁcation are summarized in Table 6. The column “TNS” (ns) and “WNS”  
(ps) denotes the total negative slack and the worst negative slack. The column “TPR” (%) and “TNR” (%) are  
the true positive and negative ratio, which represent the ratio of the number of truly critical and non-critical  
paths correctly identiﬁed by a classiﬁer versus the total number of critical and non-critical paths reported from  
PrimeTime, respectively. A perfect TPR or TNR is 1.  
As shown in Table 6, based on our accurate prediction of net delay, our “TNS” is closer to the golden result of  
PrimeTime. Furthermore, while achieving a “TPR” of 100%, our method has a much lower false alarm, resulting  
in more than 60% higher “TNR” compared to Elmore and “MLCAD’22” methods, which means our method can be  
eﬀectively used as a timing estimator for timing optimization during the placement stage.  
7.3 Timing-Driven Placement Performance Analysis  
During placement optimization, we employ the acquired model “DelayGBM” instead of the Elmore delay for  
timing evaluation. Utilizing the timing results from “DelayGBM”, pseudo-nets are added with timing slack less  
than 0 during the placement process, and the net weights are adjusted accordingly. As our timing optimization  
methodology is integrated into the open-source tool DREAMPlace, we conduct a comparison with both the  
open-source DREAMPlace [30] and net-weighting-based timing driven DREAMPlace 4.0 [29] with Elmore delay  
analysis. In performance evaluation of the ML-based model, “DAC’22” and “DelayGBM” demonstrate comparable  
accuracy in timing prediction. To assess the eﬀectiveness of the derivable “DelayGBM” model in time-driven  
optimization, we also integrate “DAC’22” with RF model as a non-derivable model in placement for a comparative  
experiment. It should be noted that the integration method for the non-derivable model “DAC’22” involves simply  
replacing the Elmore model in DREAMPlace 4.0, without the addition of pseudo-nets for driver-sink connection  
optimization on critical paths.  
Due to the small scale of ITC’99 circuits and accordingly too short wirelength, optimizing timing by reducing  
wirelength is not practical. Therefore, our primary focus is on the timing-driven optimization for larger RISC-V  
circuits, i.e., CV32, CVA6 and C910, as shown in Table 7. The “TNS” and “WNS” is consistent with the metric  
applied in [29]. Our approach “DelayGBM” achieves an obvious improvement compared to the basic version  
“DREAMPlace”. On average, it reduces TNS and WNS by 34% and 29%, respectively.  
Compared to “DREAMPlace 4.0”, “DelayGBM” achieves a 1% reduction in HPWL while improving TNS and  
WNS by 24% and 14%, respectively. This improvement can be attributed to the integration of a derivable model  
and its corresponding derivatives into the timing optimization process, which eﬀectively reduces wirelength  
performance loss while incurring signiﬁcantly less runtime overhead compared to the integration of the non-  
derivable model in “MLCAD’22” and “DAC’22”. Furthermore, we can further employ timing derivatives directly  
on driver-sink connections of critical paths. This approach results in a more signiﬁcant improvement in both  
TNS and WNS.  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
 
Eﬀicient Timing Prediction and Optimization Using Derivable Gradient Boosting Machine Model at Placement Stage

The TNS and WNS of placers are obtained according to the Elmore-based evaluation [27].  
7.4 Discussion on Derivable Timing Model  
Recent studies [20  
,
38] have also explored derivable AI models such as GNNs and Transformers for timing  
prediction. To evaluate our approach with other derivable models, we give the prediction correlation analysis on  
pin-to-pin delay and path arrival time as shown in Table 8.  
For the GNN-based timing prediction model [20], a direct comparison is not possible since it requires a speciﬁc  
library format as input for GNN embedding and has not been released for the 28 nm technology node used  
in our experiments. To address this, we re-implemented the GNN model, preserving its key components: net  
embedding and delay propagation, while adapting it to our experimental environment. Our implementation  
employs the same feature set as “delayGBM”, trained with the Adam optimizer, mixed-precision training, and  
MSE loss function.  
As shown in Table 8, compared to “delayGBM”, which achieves a pin-to-pin delay correlation of 0.96, the  
correlation of the GNN model is 0.94. The average correlation of path arrival time is also lower than delayGBM,  
especially for larger circuits. In addition, circuit graph construction for the GNN model introduces signiﬁcant  
complexity. The GNN inference time is about 20∼100x longer, averaging more than 90x longer than “delayGBM”.  
While the primary strength of GNNs lies in their ability to automatically learn graph features, this advantage may  
be less pronounced when highly correlated features are already explicitly provided, suggesting that the potential  
of GNNs in our context requires further investigation. Since the GNN model is signiﬁcantly more complex than  
“delayGBM”, we believe that a more reﬁned, GNN-speciﬁc embedding strategy is required during placement,  
which we leave for future work.  
For the Transformer model, work [38] utilizes its sequence modeling capability to predict path timing. However,  
timing paths are not simple linear chains but instead form directed acyclic graphs with branching structures.  
As a result, the method in [38] cannot be directly applied in our setting. Inspired by this work, we construct a  
Transformer-based model for pin-to-pin timing prediction. This model adopts the same features and loss function  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.

as our proposed “delayGBM”, while applying binning and encoding during preprocessing and employing a  
multi-head attention mechanism for prediction. As shown in Table 8, the Transformer model achieves an average  
delay correlation of 0.91, which is lower than the 0.96 achieved by “delayGBM”.  
Theoretically, the Transformer model can be directly integrated into the placement process. However, this would  
require developing methods to derive gradients from the Transformer and use them to guide timing optimization,  
which is beyond the scope of this work. Therefore, we instead adopt the same net-weighting integration method  
used in the “DAC’22” and “MLCAD’22” studies, and the runtime overhead of calling Transformer model is  
not accounted for a fair comparison. Using the Transformer for net weighting yields an average HPWL nearly  
identical to that of “delayGBM”. However, it produces worse timing results on average, with TNS and WNS being  
29% and 20% higher, respectively.  
7.5 Discussion on Model Application  
In recent years, with the growing research on timing prediction models, increasing attention has been paid to  
their integration into design ﬂows. During timing-driven placement, timing optimization methods can be built  
upon classical placement algorithms, where nets with timing violations receive special treatment. By adjusting net  
weights, or path constraints, timing convergence can be achieved through improved delay. This work proposes  
a derivable timing prediction model and use its derivablility to guide net weighting. Furthermore, for path  
constraints methods like [19], the derivable model can, in principle, serve as a substitute for Elmore delay in  
constructing constraint functions.  
In addition, prior works [  
4
,
15] have explored the use of timing prediction models to guide commercial EDA  
tool optimization via timing annotation commands. Unlike the timing-driven optimizations discussed above  
during placement, these approaches allow for more aggressive and ﬂexible design modiﬁcations, including buﬀer  
insertion, gate sizing, and logic restructuring.  
It should be noted that the eﬀectiveness of integrating prediction models through path constraints or tool  
commands requires further investigation. In principle, the predictions from our model or other AI-based models  
could be used with tool commands to enable more accurate timing analysis, allowing the tool to apply more  
eﬀective optimization strategies. Speciﬁcally, open questions include whether to adopt reﬁned path-margin  
annotation ratios to adjust predicted slack [  
directions will be studied in future work.  
4], apply path grouping [15], and other related strategies. These  
8
Conclusion  
In this paper, we introduce an innovative timing prediction model that can be transformed into a derivable  
function, serving as a replacement for the conventional Elmore method. To evaluate the eﬀectiveness of our  
ACM Trans. Des. Autom. Electron.  
 
Eﬀicient Timing Prediction and Optimization Using Derivable Gradient Boosting Machine Model at Placement Stage  
•
19  
timing model, we integrate it into the open-source placer DREAMPlace. We also utilize a pin-to-pin weighting  
approach based on derivable timing model is given for timing optimization. Experimental results show that  
our derivable timing prediction model can signiﬁcantly reduce the max and mean timing errors compared to  
the Elmore delay, and exhibits equivalent to or even better than previous non-derivable models. The timing  
performance after placement optimization is better than the result using Elmore delay or non-derivable model,  
i.,e., 12%∼24% smaller TNS and WNS with wirelength decreases by 1%∼5% on average.
